Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 3.2, draw the correct circuit symbol for the thermistor between A and B.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing the correct thermistor symbol between points A and B on Fig. 3.2.

Variant_1.question_text: The thermistor is at room temperature. The voltmeter reads 2.8 V. State the p.d. across the thermistor.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the answer: 2.8 V.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe how the student could find the resistance of the thermistor at 0 °C.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Place the thermistor in a beaker of melting ice (or at 0 °C).

Variant_1.question_text: The resistance at room temperature is 790 Ω and at 0 °C is 1800 Ω. State these values.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for: Resistance at room temperature is 790 Ω; Resistance at 0 °C is 1800 Ω. Ensure values are given to two significant figures if calculations are involved, but here they are directly stated.

Variant_1.question_text: What does this show about the thermistor?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: As temperature increases, resistance decreases, or vice versa.

Variant_1.question_text: State the diameter of the wire.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: 5.0 cm (± 0.1 cm).

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 4.1, draw a line from P parallel to ST to meet AB at C and label the point C.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing a line from P to meet AB that is parallel to ST by eye and labeling the point C.

Variant_1.question_text: How can the student check that the line is parallel to ST?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: Measure the distance from ST at least once (other than at P) to get the same answer, or use set squares, dividers, or protractors correctly.

Variant_1.question_text: Draw the reflected ray and label it R.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing the correct reflected ray and labeling it R.

Variant_1.question_text: Measure the angle of incidence and state its value.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: 23° ± 1°.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the refractive index of the glass block.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: 2.2 divided by the sine of the angle from part (d), so 2.2 / sin(23°).

Variant_1.question_text: Compare the calculated value with the standard value and comment.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for measuring CF and making a sensible comment, such as that the values are very close so they agree, based on the results.

Variant_2.question_text: Measure the distance D on Fig. 1.1 to the nearest millimetre. Record your result. D =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the correct answer of 4.7 cm.

Variant_2.question_text: Fig. 1.1 is drawn to a scale of one-tenth full size. Write down the actual height H of the point of support above the bench. H =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the answer of 47.0 cm, including the unit cm.

Variant_2.question_text: Complete the table in Fig. 1.2.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly completing the table with values: for h = 20.0 cm, T = 1.04 s; for h = 25.0 cm, T = 0.94 s; values must be to two decimal places as specified.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain why measuring the time for 20 oscillations, rather than for 1 oscillation, gives a more accurate value for T.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following acceptable answers: to reduce the effect of errors in starting or stopping the stopwatch; to reduce the effect of reaction time; to calculate an average; to reduce the percentage error.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 1.3, plot a graph of T squared / s2 on the y-axis against h / cm on the x-axis. Start your axes from the origin (0,0). Draw the straight line of best fit.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 4 marks as follows: Award 1 mark for axes labelled with quantity and unit, and axes correct way round; Award 1 mark for scales that are linear, not awkward, and start from (0,0); Award 1 mark for points plotted accurately; Award 1 mark for a smooth best fit straight line drawn.

Variant_2.question_text: Extend your line so that it cuts the y-axis. State the value of the intercept c on the y-axis. c =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the candidate's value to within ±0.5 small square from a correct extrapolation of the line.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the gradient m of your line. Show your working and indicate on your graph the values you use to calculate the gradient. m =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks as follows: Award 1 mark for values indicated on graph or triangle drawn and at least half the range of the plotted points (Δh ≥ 12.5); Award 1 mark for m = -0.04 ± 0.01 s2 / cm.

Variant_2.question_text: Theory suggests that H is given by the equation H = c / m. Use this equation to calculate H. H =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the candidate's c divided by m value correctly calculated.

Variant_2.question_text: Compare your measured value for H from (a)(ii) with your result in (d). State whether the two values agree with each other and justify your answer.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: YES (must be stated) and values very close or close enough or within the limits of experimental error or less than 10% difference; OR NO (must be stated) and values not close or not close enough or outside the limits of experimental error or greater than 10% difference.

Variant_2.question_text: Read the thermometer and record the temperature θ. θ =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the correct answer of 88 °C.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain why the student waits a short time before recording the temperature of the water at t = 0.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following acceptable answers: to allow thermometer to read the maximum temperature of the hot water; to give the thermometer time to respond; to wait until the temperature on thermometer stops rising; so that temperature of thermometer equals temperature of the water; to allow thermometer to reach thermal equilibrium.

Variant_2.question_text: Complete the column headings in the table in Fig. 2.4.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the column headings: time t / s, temperature θ / °C, temperature θ / °C.

Variant_2.question_text: State whether the insulation has any significant effect on the rate of cooling in the first 3.0 minutes. Justify your answer by reference to the results.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: significant change and temperature drops are not close or similar or there is a 3 °C difference; OR not significant change and temperature drops are close or similar or there is only a 3 °C difference.

Variant_2.question_text: Initially, the main cause of heat loss from the beaker is evaporation. Suggest how the student can reduce heat loss by evaporation.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: use a lid or cover the beaker.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest one way to reduce heat loss from the bottom of the beaker.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: lag the bottom of the beaker or use thicker lagging.

Variant_2.question_text: State one way in which the experiment could be improved to make the comparison fairer.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any one of: use the same volume of hot water; use the same size beaker; ensure the same room temperature; use the same time of cooling.

Variant_1.question_text: Which statement about electromagnetic waves is correct? A All electromagnetic waves have speeds in air of approximately 3 × 10⁸ m/s. B In air, some electromagnetic waves travel faster than light. C The electromagnetic waves with the largest wavelength are in the infra-red region. D The electromagnetic waves with the smallest wavelength are in the X-ray region.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: All electromagnetic waves have speeds in air of approximately 3 × 10⁸ m/s.

Variant_1.question_text: A student wishes to measure directly the circumference of a football. Which is the most suitable instrument to use? A calipers B a measuring tape C a micrometer D a ruler
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: a measuring tape.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows a satellite S travelling at a constant speed in a circular orbit around a planet P. Which statement is correct? A The resultant force on the satellite is zero. B The resultant force on the satellite is in direction B. C The resultant force on the satellite is in direction C. D The resultant force on the satellite is in direction D.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: The resultant force on the satellite is in direction D.

Variant_1.question_text: Which speed-time graph represents the motion of a railway train making a short stop at a station?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: The graph that shows the train speeding up, maintaining speed, slowing down to stop, and then speeding up again.

Variant_1.question_text: The minimum braking distance for a car is tested on a dry road. The test is then repeated on a wet road. What happens to the braking distance and to the frictional force between the tyres and the road? braking distance frictional force A decreases decreases B decreases increases C increases decreases D increases increases
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: braking distance increases and frictional force decreases.

Variant_1.question_text: Which piece of equipment is used to measure mass? A balance B manometer C measuring cylinder D newton meter
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: balance.

Variant_1.question_text: A body of mass 10 kg falling freely in the gravitational field close to the Moon’s surface has an acceleration of 1.6 m/s². What is the gravitational field strength on the Moon? A 0 N/kg B 1.6 N/kg C 10 N/kg D 16 N/kg
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: 1.6 N/kg.

Variant_1.question_text: The graph shows extension-load curves for four fibres. Which fibre is the most difficult to stretch over the range of loads shown?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: The fibre represented by the curve that shows the least extension for the given loads.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows a muscle and bones in a person’s arm. The hand holds a load of weight 40 N. The elbow acts as a pivot and the tension in the muscle keeps the lower part of the arm horizontal. What is the tension in the muscle due to the load? A 200 N B 240 N C 280 N D 1400 N
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: 280 N.

Variant_1.question_text: Four objects of equal mass rest on a table. The centre of mass of each object is labelled G. Which object is the least stable?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: The object with the centre of mass positioned in a way that makes it least stable, as indicated in the diagram.

Variant_1.question_text: Forces of 4.0 N and 2.0 N act at a point. Which scale diagram shows the forces that have a resultant of 4.0 N?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: The diagram where the forces are arranged such that their resultant is 4.0 N.

Variant_1.question_text: A student calculates his power in running up a flight of stairs. He measures the vertical height of the stairs, the time taken to run up the stairs and his weight. How does he calculate his power? A height × time × weight B time / (weight / height) C height / (weight / time) D time / height × weight
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: Power is calculated as work done divided by time, where work done is weight multiplied by height, so power = (weight × height) / time.

Variant_1.question_text: How does an oil-fired power station differ from a nuclear power station? A Gases emitted by hot fuel are emitted into the atmosphere. B Steam is produced in a boiler using hot fuel. C The hot steam is used to turn a turbine. D Turbines are used to drive an electric generator.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: Gases emitted by hot fuel are emitted into the atmosphere.

Variant_1.question_text: Five blocks have the same mass but different base areas. They all rest on a horizontal table. A graph is plotted to show the relationship between the pressure exerted on the table and the base area of the block. Which graph shows this relationship?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: The graph that shows pressure decreasing as base area increases.

Variant_1.question_text: Each tyre of a car has an area of 100 cm² in contact with the ground. The car has a mass of 1600 kg. The weight of the car is equally distributed amongst the four tyres. The gravitational field strength g is 10 N/kg. What is the pressure exerted on the ground? A 4.0 N/cm² B 16 N/cm² C 40 N/cm² D 160 N/cm²
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: 40 N/cm².

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows a simple mercury barometer. Which height is a measure of the atmospheric pressure?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: The height that corresponds to the level of mercury in the tube above the reservoir.

Variant_1.question_text: What is described as ‘the escape of more energetic molecules from the surface of a liquid’? A condensation B evaporation C melting D radiation
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: evaporation.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagrams represent four thermometers. Which thermometer has the greatest sensitivity and which thermometer has the greatest range? greatest sensitivity greatest range A P R B P S C Q R D Q S
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: The thermometer with the greatest sensitivity is Q and the greatest range is R.

Variant_1.question_text: What happens when a solid is heated and expands? A The molecules do not change size and the spaces between the molecules become larger. B The molecules expand and the spaces between the molecules become larger. C The molecules expand and the spaces between the molecules become smaller. D The molecules expand and the spaces between the molecules do not change size.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: The molecules do not change size and the spaces between the molecules become larger.

Variant_1.question_text: When a piece of smouldering rope is held at the opening of the box in the diagram, smoke moves in the direction indicated. What is responsible for the movement of the smoke? A convection B movement of free electrons C radiation D vibration of molecules
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct answer: convection.

Variant_2.question_text: Which is a vector quantity? A a mass of 2.0 kg B a temperature of –10 °C C a weight of 15 N D an average speed of 20 m / s
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which is 'a weight of 15 N'.

Variant_2.question_text: Forces of 4.0 N and 2.0 N act at a point. Which scale diagram shows the forces that have a resultant of 4.0 N? A [diagram] B [diagram] C [diagram] D [diagram]
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which shows the forces arranged such that their resultant is 4.0 N.

Variant_2.question_text: What is the name and value of the unit of power written as mW? name value A megawatt 10–3 W B megawatt 106 W C milliwatt 10–3 W D milliwatt 106 W
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which is 'milliwatt' with a value of 10–3 W.

Variant_2.question_text: Micrometers, metre rules, tapes and calipers are used for measuring lengths. Which row identifies the most suitable device for accurately measuring the stated length? length measuring device A 0.15 mm micrometer B 0.50 mm metre rule C 0.15 m tape D 0.50 m calipers
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which identifies 'micrometer' for measuring 0.15 mm.

Variant_2.question_text: The planets in the Solar System orbit the Sun. Which statement is correct? A There is a force on each planet away from the Sun. B There is a force on each planet in the direction in which it travels. C There is a force on each planet opposite to the direction in which it travels. D There is a force on each planet towards the Sun.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which states 'There is a force on each planet towards the Sun'.

Variant_2.question_text: Which forces act on a skydiver who is falling at terminal velocity? A air resistance and weight B air resistance only C weight only D no forces act
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which states 'air resistance and weight'.

Variant_2.question_text: A uniform rod of weight 5.0 N is held initially at rest. The diagram shows the forces acting on the rod when it is released. 10 N 5.0 N 2.0 N 3.0 N uniform rod What happens to the rod when it is released? A It does not move. B It moves to the right. C It moves upwards. D It starts to rotate.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which states 'It starts to rotate'.

Variant_2.question_text: A block of metal is placed on an electronic balance to record its mass. 57.0 What is the unit of the reading on the electronic balance and what is the unit of weight? unit on electronic balance unit of weight A g g B g N C N g D N N
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which states 'g' for the unit on the electronic balance and 'N' for the unit of weight.

Variant_2.question_text: A body of mass 10 kg falling freely in the gravitational field close to the Moon’s surface has an acceleration of 1.6 m / s2. What is the gravitational field strength on the Moon? A 0 N / kg B 1.6 N / kg C 10 N / kg D 16 N / kg
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is '1.6 N / kg'.

Variant_2.question_text: Two objects X and Y are suspended from a uniform rod, pivoted at its centre. The rod is in equilibrium. X Y rod pivot 10 cm 25 cm Which statement about X and Y is correct? Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which states 'The mass of X is 2.5 times the mass of Y'.

Variant_2.question_text: A force of 1600 N accelerates a car, of mass 800 kg, from rest. What is the car’s acceleration and its velocity after 4.0 s? acceleration m / s2 velocity m / s A 0.50 0.13 B 0.50 0.50 C 2.0 2.0 D 2.0 8.0
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which states acceleration of 2.0 m / s2 and velocity of 8.0 m / s.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which represents the fibre with the steepest curve, indicating it is the most difficult to stretch.

Variant_2.question_text: Four objects of equal mass rest on a table. The centre of mass of each object is labelled G. Which object is the least stable? A [diagram] B [diagram] C [diagram] D [diagram]
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which shows the object with the highest centre of mass relative to its base, making it the least stable.

Variant_2.question_text: Which power station produces carbon dioxide when operating? A gas-fired power station B geothermal power station C nuclear power station D wind power station
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which is 'gas-fired power station'.

Variant_2.question_text: Work is done when a force of 400 N pulls a crate of weight 500 N at a constant speed along a ramp, as shown. 3.0 m 5.0 m 500 N 400 N Part of the work done increases the gravitational potential energy E of the crate and the rest is work done W against friction. What are the values of E and W? E / J W / J A 1500 500 B 1500 2000 C 2000 2500 D 3500 500
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which states E as 1500 J and W as 500 J.

Variant_2.question_text: Five blocks have the same mass but different base areas. They all rest on a horizontal table. A graph is plotted to show the relationship between the pressure exerted on the table and the base area of the block. Which graph shows this relationship? pressure A [graph] B [graph] C [graph] D [graph]
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which shows pressure decreasing as base area increases.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows a simple mercury barometer. Which height is a measure of the atmospheric pressure? A [diagram] B [diagram] C [diagram] D [diagram]
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which indicates the height of the mercury column corresponding to atmospheric pressure.

Variant_2.question_text: A sealed packet containing air and a snack is purchased at an airport. The sealed packet is taken on board an aircraft. During the flight the packet becomes larger. What causes the packet to become larger? A The density of the air inside the packet increases. B The mass of the packet increases. C The pressure of the air outside the packet decreases. D The volume of the air inside the packet decreases.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which states 'The pressure of the air outside the packet decreases'.

Variant_2.question_text: A sealed container of gas is heated and the pressure inside increases. What happens to the molecules of the gas to cause this increase in pressure? A Their kinetic energy decreases. B They become heavier. C They expand. D They hit the container more frequently.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which states 'They hit the container more frequently'.

Variant_2.question_text: Equal masses of copper and water are heated to the same temperature. As they cool down, the copper and the water lose thermal energy at the same rate. The temperature of the copper falls faster. Why is this? A Copper has a larger specific heat capacity. B Copper has a larger specific latent heat. C Copper has a smaller specific heat capacity. D Copper has a smaller specific latent heat.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which states 'Copper has a smaller specific heat capacity'.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows a thermometer. column of mercury capillary tube X What is in region X of the capillary tube? A a vacuum B alcohol C steam D water
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which is 'a vacuum'.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagrams represent four thermometers. P 25 °C 20 °C 15 °C 10 °C 5 °C 0 °C Q 40 °C 35 °C 30 °C R 250 °C 200 °C 150 °C 100 °C S 500 °C 400 °C 300 °C 200 °C 100 °C 0 °C Which thermometer has the widest range of temperature? A P B Q C R D S
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which is thermometer R with the widest range from 0 °C to 250 °C.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe how the average speed of the cyclist can be measured. In your account, suggest what equipment is used, the measurements that are made and how the average speed is calculated.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: measuring the distance (between two marks) using a metre rule or tape measure. Award 1 mark for: measuring the time (between two marks) using a stopwatch, stopclock, or timer. Award 1 mark for: calculating the average speed as the total distance divided by the time.

Variant_1.question_text: State what is meant by a negative acceleration.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: a decrease in speed or deceleration.

Variant_1.question_text: The size of the acceleration is 1.8 m / s2. Calculate the change in speed of the cyclist in a time of 2.5 s.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: using the formula change in speed equals acceleration multiplied by time, which is 1.8 m/s2 multiplied by 2.5 s. Award 1 mark for: the correct calculation resulting in 4.5 m/s.

Variant_1.question_text: State what is meant by balanced forces.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: equal and opposite forces or no resultant force.

Variant_1.question_text: The frictional force in Fig. 2.1 does not produce any heating effect. State what must happen for the frictional force to produce heating.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: the box moves or slides.

Variant_1.question_text: Apart from being stationary, describe one other possible state of motion of the box when the forces are balanced.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: constant velocity or constant speed and direction.

Variant_1.question_text: When P = 100 N and F = 85 N, the box accelerates. The mass of the box is 25 kg. Calculate the acceleration of the box.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: identifying the resultant force as 15 N. Award 1 mark for: using the formula acceleration equals resultant force divided by mass. Award 1 mark for: the correct calculation resulting in 0.60 m/s2.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain how the molecules of air in the bag exert a pressure on the inside of the bag.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: the molecules hit the inside wall. Award 1 mark for: the molecules create a force and this force is related to the area.

Variant_1.question_text: When the aircraft is on the ground, the pressure of the air inside the aircraft is 100 kPa. Calculate the volume of air inside the bag when the aircraft is on the ground.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: using the formula p1 V1 equals p2 V2. Award 1 mark for: the correct calculation resulting in 400 cm3.

Variant_1.question_text: State two assumptions that you made in your calculation in (b)(i).
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark each for any two of the following: no gas or air or molecules escape; temperature is constant; no forces between molecules.

Variant_1.question_text: Define what is meant by work done.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: force multiplied by distance moved. Award 1 mark for: force multiplied by distance moved in the direction of the force.

Variant_2.question_text: On the grid in Fig. 1.2, plot a distance-time graph for the object.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for plotting the points correctly at (1,0.5), (2,1.6), (3,3), (4,5), and (5,7). Award 1 mark for drawing a smooth curve from the origin.

Variant_2.question_text: State and explain how a distance-time graph shows that speed is constant.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the graph is a straight line or that the gradient or slope is constant. Award 1 mark for explaining that this means the object travels equal distances in the same time or that the gradient equals speed.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain, in terms of the forces acting on the object, how it is able to fall at a constant speed.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for identifying the forces as weight and the upward force from the oil or liquid. Award 1 mark for explaining that these forces are balanced, cancel, are equal and opposite, or that there is no resultant force.

Variant_2.question_text: Only some of the extensions are shown in the table. Complete the table to show all of the extensions.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for completing the table with the values 150 mm and 220 mm for the extensions corresponding to loads of 3.0 N and 4.0 N respectively.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the load that produces an extension of 49 mm.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award credit for any use of proportionality between load and extension. Award 1 mark for the correct calculation leading to a load of 1.4 N.

Variant_2.question_text: The load is stationary at A and at B. The load has no kinetic energy at either point. Place ticks (✓) in the boxes to show how the value of each of the forms of energy compares at A and B.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly indicating that the energy stored in the spring is larger at B and that the gravitational potential energy of the load is larger at A.

Variant_2.question_text: Work is done by the student to pull the load down. The law of conservation of energy states that energy cannot be created and cannot be destroyed. Explain how this principle applies in this case.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the student loses energy or that chemical energy decreases, and that gravitational potential energy decreases while spring energy increases. Award 1 mark for explaining that the increase in spring energy is greater than the decrease in gravitational potential energy, so the student provides the additional work needed.

Variant_2.question_text: Equal volumes of metal, water and air are heated from 20 °C to 80 °C. State whether the metal, the water or the air expands the most.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the air expands the most.

Variant_2.question_text: Fig. 3.1 is a diagram of the metal at 20 °C. The metal is a large, square sheet with a square hole at its centre. The sheet lies flat on a table. On Fig. 3.1, draw the outline of the sheet and of the hole after the metal is heated. Make sure that the expansion is noticeable.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing the square sheet larger. Award 1 mark for drawing the hole larger.

Variant_2.question_text: The metal has a mass of 5.0 kg and a density of 7.5 × 10^3 kg / m^3. Define density.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for defining density as mass divided by volume or mass per unit volume.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the volume of the metal. Give your answer to a suitable number of significant figures.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award credit for using the formula volume equals mass divided by density or rearranging algebraically or numerically to find volume. Award 1 mark for the correct calculation leading to 6.7 × 10^{-4} m^3 or 6.67 × 10^{-4} m^3.

Variant_2.question_text: One form of latent heat is the thermal energy needed to melt a solid. Define specific latent heat.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is C: 4.0 s, based on power and efficiency calculations.

Variant_2.question_text: Different liquids are poured into four different containers. In which container does the liquid produce the greatest pressure at the bottom of the container? area of base / cm2 density of liquid / g/cm3 depth of liquid / cm A 10 1.3 50 B 20 0.80 80 C 40 1.0 60 D 50 0.92 75
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is D: the container with the greatest pressure at the bottom.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option, which is B: 0.30 N / cm2, the smallest pressure based on the surface area.

Variant_2.question_text: A long tube, full of mercury, is inverted in a small dish of mercury. The mercury level in the tube falls, leaving a vacuum at the top. When the atmospheric pressure decreases, which length decreases? A PQ B PS C QR D RS

Variant_1.question_text: Complete the equation for the kinetic energy of an object travelling with a speed v and state what any other terms in the equation represent.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the increase in the internal energy of the rock.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly using the formula ∆Q = m c ∆T and substituting the values (mass = 0.60 kg, specific heat capacity c = 560 J/(kg °C), and temperature change ∆T = 25 °C). Award 1 mark for the correct answer of 8.4 × 10³ J.

Variant_1.question_text: Use the answer from (b)(i) to determine the minimum possible speed of the rock when it hits the Earth.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly using the equation v² = 2 ∆Q / m or equivalent substitution. Award 1 mark for the correct answer of 167 m/s.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest one reason why the speed determined in (b)(ii) is less than the actual speed of the rock when it hits the Earth.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting a reason such as some internal or thermal energy is lost to the ground or air, or work is done to shatter the rock, or the rock bounces, or energy is used to compress the ground, or the rock melts.

Variant_2.question_text: State how speed differs from velocity.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: stating that speed does not have a direction or that speed is a scalar quantity.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain, in terms of its velocity, why the satellite is accelerating.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: stating that the direction of velocity changes as it moves around the Earth. Award another mark for: stating that its velocity changes with time, which means it is accelerating.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 1.1, draw an arrow, starting on the satellite, to show the direction of the satellite’s acceleration.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: drawing an arrow from the satellite towards the centre of the Earth.

Variant_2.question_text: State and explain whether this force does work on the satellite and state whether the energy of the satellite is affected.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: stating that no work is done because the force is perpendicular to the motion or there is no movement in the direction of the force. Award another mark for: stating that the energy of the satellite remains constant or that its kinetic and gravitational potential energy are not affected.

Variant_2.question_text: State what is meant by centre of mass.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: stating that the centre of mass is the place where the entire mass seems to be located.

Variant_2.question_text: State two features of the design that make the car in Fig. 2.1 stable.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: stating that the car has a wide base or a large base area. Award another mark for: stating that the car has a low centre of mass.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how to determine the position of the centre of mass of the piece of card.

Award another mark for: marking a vertical line on the lamina along the string or plumb line. Award a third mark for: repeating the process from another point and stating that the centre of mass is at the intersection of the lines.

Variant_1.question_text: Make your own version of exhibit A: Cut a piece of adhesive tape 20 cm long. Stick 15 cm of this length of tape on to the graph paper. Fold the other 5 cm of tape around the end of the loop of string opposite the spring. Press the end of the tape around the loop to seal the loop inside, as shown in Fig. 1.1. Use another piece of tape to fix the left-hand edge of the paper to the bench.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly setting up the apparatus as described, including cutting the tape, sticking 15 cm on the graph paper, folding and sealing the other 5 cm around the loop of string, and fixing the paper to the bench.

Variant_1.question_text: For the spring provided, measure the unstretched length lA of the coiled part of the spring.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a measured unstretched length lA between 1.8 cm and 2.2 cm.

Variant_1.question_text: Pull the spring carefully and slowly to the right. As you pull, keep the spring very close to the bench, but not touching it. The spring stretches. At some point the adhesive tape will just begin to peel away from the paper. Hold the spring steady in this position.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: This part is procedural and not directly marked in the provided scheme. No specific marks allocated.

Variant_1.question_text: Measure the stretched length lB of the coiled part of the spring. Use the equation e = lB – lA to calculate the extension e of the spring.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a calculated extension e where the stretched length lB is between 4.0 cm and 11.0 cm, and e is obtained by subtracting the value of lA from lB.

Variant_1.question_text: Use the equation F = ke to calculate the force F applied by the spring, where k = 0.25 N / cm. Give the unit of your answer.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating F as 0.25 multiplied by the extension e from part (c)(ii), and including the unit newtons (N).

Variant_1.question_text: Repeat c(i). As soon as the adhesive tape begins to peel away from the paper, start the stopwatch. Continue pulling and maintain a constant extension of the spring until 3.0 cm of the tape has been peeled away from the paper. Stop the stopwatch. Record the time t taken to peel 3.0 cm of the tape away from the paper.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a recorded time t between 2 seconds and 50 seconds, given to one decimal place.

Variant_1.question_text: Use the equation P = 0.030F / t to calculate the power P developed.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating P as (0.030 multiplied by the force F from part (c)(iii)) divided by the time t from part (d), with the unit watts (W).

Variant_1.question_text: Adjust the height of the clamp holding the lens until a sharply focussed image of the filament of the lamp is formed on the sheet of graph paper. Measure the distance u from the filament of the lamp to the lens.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a measured distance u between 16.5 cm and 20 cm.

Variant_1.question_text: Measure the distance v from the lens to the graph paper.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a measured distance v that is 115 cm minus the value of u from part (a)(i).

Variant_1.question_text: Describe one difficulty you had in measuring these distances.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for describing one suitable difficulty, such as ensuring the ruler is vertical, avoiding parallax error, identifying the exact position of the filament or center of the lens, keeping the metre rule still, or the effect of the lens on magnifying or diminishing the bulb.

Variant_1.question_text: Use the equation f = uv / (u + v) to calculate the focal length f of the lens.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 2 marks for a focal length f in the range 14.1 cm to 15.6 cm. Award 1 mark for f in the range 13.1 cm to 16.1 cm but not within 14.1 to 15.6 cm.

Variant_1.question_text: Determine the mass m1 of the empty measuring cylinder.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a mass m1 that is the supervisor's value plus or minus 5 grams.

Variant_1.question_text: Pour 100 cm3 of cooking oil from the beaker into the measuring cylinder. Describe how you took an accurate reading of the volume of oil in the measuring cylinder. You may draw a diagram.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for describing that the eye should be level with the scale and the bottom of the meniscus, or providing a diagram showing the eye at right angles to the base of the meniscus and the scale reading.

Variant_1.question_text: Determine the mass m2 of the measuring cylinder containing 100 cm3 of cooking oil.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for m2 in the range 87 g to 95 g plus the value of m1 from part (a), and then m as m2 minus m1.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the mass m of the cooking oil.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: See the marking for 3(c)(i), as it is combined: Award 1 mark for calculating m as m2 minus m1, where m2 is from (c)(i) and m1 from (a).

Variant_2.question_text: Pull the spring slowly to the right as shown in Fig. 1.1. The spring stretches, as shown in Fig. 1.2, and the block just begins to slide. Hold the spring steady in this position. Measure the stretched length ls of the spring at this position. ls =  ..................................................... cm
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the measured stretched length ls is between 4.5 cm and 15.49 cm. Acceptable answers: Any value in the range 4.5 to 15.49 cm.

Variant_2.question_text: For the spring provided, measure the unstretched length lu of the spring, as shown in Fig. 1.3. lu =  .................................................... cm Use the equation e = ls – lu to calculate the extension e of the spring. e =  .................................................... cm
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the measured unstretched length lu is between 1.8 cm and 2.2 cm, and the extension e is calculated by subtracting lu from the value of ls obtained in part (a)(i). Acceptable answers: lu between 1.8 cm and 2.2 cm; e = ls - lu, where ls is the value from (a)(i).

Variant_2.question_text: Use the equation F = ke to calculate the force F applied by the spring, where k = 0.25 N / cm. Give the unit of your answer. F = ................................ unit ...................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the force F is calculated as the extension e from part (a)(ii) multiplied by 0.25 N/cm, and the unit is newtons (N). Acceptable answers: F = e × 0.25, where e is from (a)(ii); unit is N.

Variant_2.question_text: Pull the spring slowly to the right until the block just begins to slide on the bench. Start the stopwatch and pull the block along the bench, keeping the extension of the spring constant. Stop the stopwatch when the block has moved 20 cm. Repeat and find the average value t for the time taken. Show your working. t =  ....................................................... s
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if at least two sensible time values are recorded and averaged to find t. Acceptable answers: At least two measurements of time taken for the block to move 20 cm, with the average calculated correctly.

Variant_2.question_text: Use the equation P = 0.20 F / t to calculate the power P developed. P =  ...................................................... W
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the power P is calculated using P = 0.20 × F from part (a)(iii) divided by t from part (b). Acceptable answers: P = (0.20 × F) / t, where F is from (a)(iii) and t is from (b).

Variant_2.question_text: Connect one filament lamp between points P and Q. Switch on the circuit. Record the potential difference V and the current I in the table of Fig. 2.2. Switch off.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the potential difference V is between 1.5 V and 2.4 V, and the current I is between 0.1 A and 0.3 A. Acceptable answers: V in the range 1.5 to 2.4 V; I in the range 0.1 to 0.3 A.

Variant_2.question_text: Repeat the procedure in (a) with: 2 filament lamps in series; 2 filament lamps in parallel; 3 filament lamps in parallel. Switch off the circuit when you have completed your measurements.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks as follows: Award 1 mark if for 2 lamps in series, V is greater than in (a) and I is less than in (a). Award 1 mark if for 2 lamps in parallel, V is less than in (a) and I is greater than in (a), and for 3 lamps in parallel, V is higher and I is lower than for 2 lamps in parallel. Acceptable answers: For 2 lamps in series: V > value from (a), I < value from (a); For 2 lamps in parallel: V < value from (a), I > value from (a); For 3 lamps in parallel: V higher than 2 lamps in parallel, I lower than 2 lamps in parallel.

Variant_2.question_text: Use the equation RL = V / I to calculate the resistance RL of each arrangement. Record your answers in the table of Fig. 2.2.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the resistance RL is correctly calculated for each arrangement using RL = V / I from the values recorded in parts (a) and (b). Acceptable answers: RL = V divided by I for each case, with correct units in ohms (Ω).

Variant_2.question_text: When the circuit with a single filament lamp is switched on, the ammeter reading rises. The reading then decreases very slightly to a constant value within a very short period of time. Suggest why the reading decreases.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the explanation is that the resistance of the lamp or filament increases as the current heats the wire and the lamp gets hot. Acceptable answers: The resistance of the filament increases because the current heats the wire as the lamp gets hot.

Variant_2.question_text: Check that the filament of the lamp is at the 0.0 cm mark on the rule and that the screen is at the 100.0 cm mark on the rule so that the distance between the filament and the screen is 100.0 cm. The filament should be perpendicular to the metre rule and parallel to the screen, as shown in Fig. 3.2. Switch the lamp on. Move the lens slowly towards the lamp until a sharply focussed magnified image of the filament is formed on the screen. On Fig. 3.2, mark the distance x from the filament to the lens.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the distance x is marked on the diagram between the lamp and the lens, and the measured value is between 15 cm and 25 cm to the nearest millimetre. Acceptable answers: x marked correctly on Fig. 3.2; measured x between 15 cm and 25 cm, recorded to nearest mm.

Variant_2.question_text: On the apparatus, measure the distance x between the filament and the lens. x =  .................................................... cm
Variant_2.marking_scheme: This is combined with (a)(i) in the mark scheme, but for this sub-part: Award the mark as part of the overall for (a)(i)/(ii). Refer to the full scheme: x on diagram between the lamp and lens and 25 ≥ answer ≥ 15 cm to nearest mm. Acceptable answers: Measured x between 15 cm and 25 cm, to nearest mm.

Variant_2.question_text: Measure the length li of the image of the filament formed on the screen. li =  .................................................... cm
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the length li of the image is between 1.0 cm and 3.0 cm. Acceptable answers: li between 1.0 cm and 3.0 cm.

Variant_2.question_text: The filament of the lamp is shown in Fig. 3.3. Measure the length lo for the filament lamp provided. lo =  ......................................................... cm
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if lo is present and realistic, as part of (b)(i) and (ii). Acceptable answers: A realistic value for lo measured from Fig. 3.3.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest why your measurement of lo may not be accurate.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the suggestion is one of: Not able to measure lo with rule touching filament; glass bulb prevents ruler from touching filament; possible parallax error when reading scale with qualification; glass bulb may distort apparent length of filament. Acceptable answers: The measurement may not be accurate because the glass bulb prevents the ruler from touching the filament, or due to parallax error when reading the scale, or the glass bulb distorts the apparent length.

Variant_2.question_text: Use the equation m = li / lo to calculate the magnification m produced by the lens. m =  ...................
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if the magnification m is calculated as li from (a)(iii) divided by lo from (b)(i). Acceptable answers: m = li / lo, using values from previous parts.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest why the water is left to stand for ten minutes before placing the crystal in the tube.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following acceptable answers: moving or disturbed water would cause the colour to spread; or allows the water to settle or be still. Not accepted: Any other responses.

Variant_1.question_text: When the water is heated, it is heated gently. Suggest why.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following acceptable answers: water might boil; or the colour would spread throughout too quickly; or allows steady movement of water; or allows the colour to move slowly; or to be able to observe the colour changes; or able to see the convection current; or able to see water moving. Not accepted: Any other responses.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.1, draw an arrow to show the direction in which the purple colour moves through the water.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: a clockwise arrow drawn on Fig. 1.1 to indicate the direction of the purple colour movement. Not accepted: Any incorrect or incomplete arrow.

Variant_1.question_text: State one safety precaution the teacher should take in this demonstration.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any sensible safety precaution, such as: wearing goggles; using a safety screen; or wearing gloves while handling the crystal. Not accepted: Precautions not related to the demonstration.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 2.1, show the height h.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: correctly indicating the length h on Fig. 2.1 as the vertical height from the bench to the release point of the metal ball. Not accepted: Incorrect or misplaced indication.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 2.1, show the distance d.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: correctly indicating the length d on Fig. 2.1 as the distance the glass ball travels along the bench after collision. Not accepted: Incorrect or misplaced indication.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest a piece of apparatus to measure these two lengths.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: metre rule. Not accepted: Any other apparatus not suitable for measuring lengths.

Variant_1.question_text: The experiment is repeated. The glass ball travels the following distances: 23.5 cm, 20.8 cm, 22.3 cm. Calculate the average distance dav travelled.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: calculating the average distance as (23.5 + 20.8 + 22.3) divided by 3, which equals 22.2 cm or 22 cm. Acceptable answers: 22.2 cm or 22 cm. Not accepted: Incorrect calculations or units.

Variant_1.question_text: The initial gravitational potential energy E of the metal ball at the top of the slope is given by E = mgh, where g is 10 N/kg and m is the mass of the metal ball. The metal ball has a mass m of 0.0081 kg. The height h of the ramp is 0.120 m. Calculate E, giving your answer to 2 significant figures.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 2 marks for: first, calculating E = m × g × h = 0.0081 × 10 × 0.120 = 0.00972 J; then rounding to 2 significant figures as 0.0097 J or 9.7 × 10^{-3} J. Part A: Calculation of 0.00972 J (1 mark). Part B: Rounding to 0.0097 J (1 mark). Not accepted: Incorrect values or significant figures.

Variant_1.question_text: Complete the table for the metal ball of mass 0.0081 kg using your answers to (a)(iv) and (a)(v).
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award marks as per the table completion: For E / J, use the value from 2(a)(v) which is 0.0097 J (to 2 significant figures); for dav / cm, use the value from 2(a)(iv) which is 22.2 cm or 22 cm. The completed row should be: mass 0.0081 kg, E 0.0097 J, dav 22.2 cm or 22 cm. Not accepted: Incorrect values from previous parts.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the candidate's value read from the graph at 3.6 N; Award 1 mark for subtracting the candidate's value of l0 from this reading.

Variant_2.question_text: On the axes in Fig. 1.3, sketch a graph of L against e.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for sketching a straight line through the origin.

Variant_2.question_text: The gradient of your graph in Fig. 1.2 measures the force constant of the spring. The greater the force constant, the harder it is to stretch the spring. A second spring has the same unstretched length but a greater force constant. Using the same axes, draw on Fig. 1.2 a line to show how the length of this second spring changes as loads are added to it. Label this line S.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a line starting at the same initial value of l; Award 1 mark for a straight line with a steeper slope than the original.

Variant_2.question_text: Record the mass m of the test-tube to three significant figures. m =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for recording the mass as 13.6 g.

Variant_2.question_text: Read and record the new water level V2 in the measuring cylinder. V2 =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for recording the water level as 79.0 cm³.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the increase in the volume reading V in the measuring cylinder. V =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating V as 14.0 cm³.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest one source of experimental inaccuracy in reading the scale on the measuring cylinder.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting not reading perpendicular to the scale or at eye level, or not reading to the bottom of the meniscus, or that the measuring cylinder only reads to 1 or 2 cm³.

Variant_2.question_text: Use your answers to (a) and (b)(ii) to calculate ρ. State the unit of your answer. ρ =
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating ρ as 0.971; Award 1 mark for stating the unit as g/cm³.

Variant_2.question_text: Read the voltmeter scale and record your answer in Table 3.1.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for reading the voltmeter as 0.21 V.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the resistance of a length l = 10.0 cm of the wire. Use the equation R = V / I. Record your answer in Table 3.1.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating the resistance as 0.84 Ω.

Variant_2.question_text: Look at the results in Table 3.1. Within the limits of experimental error, suggest the relationship between l and R.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for as l increases, R increases; Award 1 mark for stating that l and R are directly proportional, or that l / R is constant, or that doubling l doubles R.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe one precaution that the student can take to minimise this heating effect.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for describing to switch off between readings, or use smaller currents or voltages, or use a fan.
